title: Optical squeezing mediated by levitated oscillators at their quantum ground state
authors: A. Pontin, Q. Deplano, F. Marino, F. Marin
abstract: We demonstrate optical squeezing below the shot-noise level generated through the interaction of an optical cavity field with two center-of-mass modes of a levitated nanoparticle, simultaneously cooled to occupation numbers well below unity. By analyzing the quadrature fluctuations of the cavity output through heterodyne detection, we resolve the full spectral covariance matrix of the optical field and map regions of sub-shot-noise squeezing as a function of detection phase and frequency. Operating in the resolved sideband and strong coupling regime where mechanical modes hybridize with the optical mode, we observe consistent squeezing in the band 70-95 kHz with a lowest variance of 0.98 (2$\%$ below vacuum fluctuations). We thus demonstrate optical squeezing mediated by multiple mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground state, bridging mechanical quantum control with non-classical light and establishing levitated optomechanics as a platform for multimode quantum interactions.
summary: This paper reports the first demonstration of optical squeezing mediated by two mechanical oscillators that are simultaneously cooled to their quantum ground state. Using a levitated nanoparticle in an optical cavity, the researchers observed squeezing 2% below the shot-noise level. The study successfully accounts for experimental phase noise to match theoretical predictions. This achievement bridges mechanical quantum control with the generation of non-classical light, establishing a new platform for multi-mode optomechanical quantum interfaces.
main_problem: The challenge of demonstrating optical squeezing mediated by mechanical oscillators that are specifically prepared in their quantum ground state, while overcoming the mutually exclusive regimes of traditional optomechanics.
main_result: Demonstration of optical squeezing below the shot-noise level (2% below vacuum fluctuations) mediated by two mechanical modes in their quantum ground state.
method: The researchers used balanced heterodyne detection and a numerical lock-in amplifier to reconstruct the full spectral covariance matrix of the optical field, incorporating a phase noise model to achieve agreement with experimental data.
model_or_system: A levitated silica nanoparticle in an optical tweezer placed inside a high-finesse optical cavity, operating in the resolved sideband and strong coupling regimes where two transverse mechanical modes hybridize with the optical mode.
key_observables: Quadrature fluctuations, spectral covariance matrix elements (S_QQ, S_PP, S_QP, S_PQ), power spectral densities, and occupation numbers.
important_parameters: Mechanical frequencies (approx. 121 kHz and 109 kHz), occupation numbers (n < 1), cavity linewidth (57 kHz), and detection efficiency (0.32).
main_assumptions: The model assumes the nanosphere is in its quantum ground state and uses a Gaussian-distributed fluctuating phase to account for residual experimental phase noise.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 displays the reconstructed spectral covariance matrix; Figure 3 presents 2D maps of the optical quadrature spectrum; Figure 4 compares the optimal quadrature spectrum against models with and without phase noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and the problem of ground-state mediated squeezing, describes the optomechanical platform and detection methods, presents the reconstruction of the covariance matrix and the observation of squeezing, and concludes with a discussion on modeling phase noise and future directions.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates the control of non-classical light through the hybridization of multiple mechanical modes in the quantum regime, providing a platform for multi-mode quantum interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
